full_response:
We regard one “turn’’ of the game as one simultaneous toss of the two fair six–sided dice.

1.  Probabilities of the relevant outcomes of a single turn  

   good (Asami immediately wins) :      P(sum = 5) = 4/36 = 1/9  
   bad  (Bolin immediately wins) :      P(sum ≥ 8) = 15/36 = 5/12  
   neutral (game continues)       :      P(sum ∈ {2,3,4,6,7}) = 17/36  

   Check: 1/9 + 5/12 + 17/36 = 36/36 = 1.

2.  Define the events  

   Eₙ  := “the first (n–1) turns are all neutral and the n-th turn is a 5’’,
          n = 1,2,3,…

   Formally, with Ni = {2,3,4,6,7} and F = {5},  

        Eₙ = { (X₁,…,Xₙ,…) : X₁,…,X_{n-1} ∈ Ni,  Xₙ ∈ F }.

3.  Probability of Eₙ  

   Independence of the turns implies  

        P[Eₙ] = (17/36)^{\,n-1} · (1/9).         (∗)

4.  Why we may sum the probabilities  

   •  The events {Eₙ}ₙ≥1 are pairwise disjoint:  
      If Eₙ occurs, the first 5 appears at time n; it cannot
      simultaneously appear first at any other time m ≠ n.

   •  Their union is exactly “Asami wins’’:  
      Whenever Asami wins, there is a unique time n at which the first 5
      appears before any sum ≥ 8, hence the outcome lies in Eₙ.  
      Conversely, any outcome in some Eₙ gives Asami the win.

   Therefore,
        {Asami wins} = ⋃_{n=1}^{∞} Eₙ,
   and by countable additivity of probability for disjoint events
        P(Asami wins) = Σ_{n=1}^{∞} P[Eₙ].

   Summing would be illegal only if the events were not disjoint or did
   not cover the whole winning set; here both properties hold.

5.  Summation  

   Using (∗),
        Σ_{n=1}^{∞} P[Eₙ]
        = (1/9) · Σ_{n=0}^{∞} (17/36)^{\,n}
        = (1/9) · 1 / (1 − 17/36)          (geometric series, |17/36|<1)
        = (1/9) · 1 / (19/36)
        = (1/9) · 36/19
        = 4/19.

Thus the probability that Asami wins the game is 4/19.

\boxed{\displaystyle \Pr[\text{Asami wins}] = \frac{4}{19}}

Confidence: 0.82
